# Reading social cues {#sec-social}

The previous chapter ended on a negative with teeth: no self-supervised scoring function
discovers which frame–utterance pairs are aligned, because at BabyView's referential floor
the co-occurrence signal is too sparse to bootstrap from. The missing ingredient is the
external structure a child actually has — a caregiver's gaze and pointing, joint attention,
the prosody of child-directed speech — that *supplies* reference rather than requiring it to
be discovered [@frank2013social]. This chapter asks two concrete questions about that
structure: *how good* would such a cue have to be to ignite the loop, and *is any such cue
actually recoverable* from this data stream?

## How much information would ignite it? {#sec-titrate}

The six mechanisms all put the same question to the data and get the same answer — the
endogenous signal is too weak — but they do not say *how* weak, relative to what would be
enough. We can put a number on the target by replacing the self-estimated alignment score
with a **synthetic cue of controlled quality**: a rank-Gaussian corruption of the held-out
CLIP labels with a tunable Spearman correlation $\rho$ to the truth. Sweeping $\rho$ holds
the model, data, and training loop fixed and varies only the informativeness of the
alignment signal — a direct titration of the target.

*How* the cue is used turns out to matter as much as its quality. A **soft** cue enters as
a graded per-pair weight in the contrastive loss: every pair still trains, scaled by how
aligned the cue believes it is. A **hard** cue is thresholded — keep the top fraction (here
12%, the aligned base rate) as positives, drop the rest to a floor. The hard version is the
faithful analogue of the Chapter 3 CLIP filter, which keeps `clip > 0.24` and discards the
rest.

| cue quality $\rho$ | resulting filter precision | 4AFC |
|--------------------|----------------------------|------|
| 0.1 | 0.16 | 32.5 |
| 0.3 | 0.24 | 34.6 |
| 0.5 | 0.35 | 38.0 |
| 0.7 | 0.49 | 39.5 |
| 1.0 | 1.00 | 41.7 |

*(Hard-gate mode; unweighted baseline 34.8, region-MIL oracle 49.9. The gate ceiling of
41.7 at $\rho = 1$ falls short of the plain oracle because it still leaks a 0.05 weight
floor onto negatives and runs the EM regime rather than clean contrastive training on the
aligned set.)*

Three things follow.

**There is an ignition threshold, near $\rho \approx 0.3$–$0.5$.** Below it — a cue that
picks pairs barely above the base rate — hard filtering is useless or actively *harmful*:
at $\rho = 0.1$ the gate keeps a near-random 12% subset and drops *below* baseline, having
discarded most of the usable data for no gain in purity. Above it, accuracy climbs. In
precision terms the cue must enrich the aligned base rate by **2–3×** (12% → ~25–35%) before
it buys anything.

**A soft cue cannot substitute for a hard one.** Used as a graded weight, even a *perfect*
cue ($\rho = 1.0$) reaches only 37.8 — short of the 41.7 the same cue delivers when
thresholded, and further still from the oracle. Contrastive learning is dragged by the
pairs it does not fully exclude; a confident belief that a pair is misaligned must be
*acted on* (the pair dropped), not merely down-weighted. Any real cue therefore has to be
committed to a discrete decision, not folded in as a soft prior.

**The loop does not amplify a toehold.** A third mode seeds *only* the warm-up with the cue
and then hands control back to the endogenous EM. If self-training had any amplification
gain — the "memorisation toehold" that noisy-correspondence methods rely on — a seeded
start would compound into a better endogenous filter. It does not: the seeded runs decay
straight back to the unweighted baseline (34.8), because once EM recomputes weights from the
model's own $\rho \le 0.1$ scores the injected signal washes out. This is the mechanism
behind every negative result above. The bootstrap never stalled for want of a lucky start;
it stalls because there is no gain in the loop to turn a weak signal into a strong one. The
alignment information has to be *supplied and maintained* from outside, at $\rho \gtrsim
0.3$.

## Can we recover the cues children use? {#sec-cue-audit}

The titration sets a bar; the question is whether any signal actually present in the
BabyView stream clears it. Children have cues that this corpus's pixels and words do not
obviously carry — a caregiver's gaze and pointing, the prosody of child-directed speech —
and some machine-readable proxies are computable now. We score each with the same yardstick
used throughout: its Spearman correlation with held-out CLIP alignment.

From the person and object detections (available for 38% of pairs) we take **hands**
(present, count, near-contact with an object), **people** (presence and apparent closeness
via box area), and scene clutter. From adjacent 1fps frame embeddings we take **head
stability** (cosine between neighbouring frames — a proxy for the head motion the IMU also
records). From the audio we take **prosody** over each utterance window: the energy and
pitch dynamics that mark child-directed speech.

| cue | $\rho$ vs CLIP alignment |
|-----|--------------------------|
| hands (present / count / contact) | ~0.005 |
| people, scene clutter | −0.04 to −0.10 |
| head stability | −0.156 |
| *vision cues combined* | *0.136 (AUC 0.539)* |
| prosody: pitch range | 0.107 |
| prosody: energy range | 0.103 |
| *prosody combined* | *0.147 (AUC 0.607)* |

The result is a clean negative with one instructive exception. **Hands — the most intuitive
social cue — carry essentially no alignment information** ($\rho \approx 0.005$), for a
structural reason: in infant egocentric video a hand is almost always in frame (the child's
own, or a caregiver's), so hand *presence* cannot separate referential from non-referential
moments, and hand–object contact from coarse boxes at 1fps is too blunt to say *which*
object is meant. Head stability is the only visual cue with any signal, and it is weak and
*negative* — stiller moments are slightly *less* aligned, the opposite of the held-object
intuition.

**Prosody is the best channel**: its cues are all positive and it separates aligned pairs
better than vision (AUC 0.607 vs 0.539), echoing the language-over-vision asymmetry that
recurred through the bootstrap experiments. The child-directed-speech emphasis proxies
(pitch and energy range) reach $\rho \approx 0.10$ from an independent, language-side
channel. (Utterance *duration* correlates more strongly, $\rho = 0.17$, but partly as an
artefact: the CLIP label is a maximum over the utterance's frames, so longer utterances
score higher mechanically.)

But no channel clears the bar. The best combination of real cues reaches $\rho \approx
0.14$ — above the best *endogenous* bootstrap signal (0.095), so social cues do carry more
than self-supervision, yet still only a third to a half of the $\rho \gtrsim 0.3$ the
titration demands. The cues we can currently read are real but too weak, by a factor of two
to three.

## What is missing, quantified {#sec-missing}

Together the two analyses close the Chapter 3 argument with a number. Alignment filtering
works when the filter is good; a *self-supervised* filter never gets good, because the loop
has no amplification gain; and the *social* cues that could supply the filter from outside —
as they do for a child — are, in their currently machine-readable forms, two to three times
too weak to ignite it. The negative result of the six mechanisms is therefore not a
modelling shortfall awaiting a seventh trick. It is a measurement: at BabyView's referential
floor, the alignment signal available to a passive learner — from co-occurrence, from hands,
from head motion, even from prosody — sits below the threshold at which any filter, learned
or given, begins to help.

The gap is specific, and it points somewhere concrete. Every cue that failed measured
*presence* — is a hand in frame, is the scene still. The cue the joint-attention literature
says matters is *direction*: the vector from a caregiver's gaze or pointing arm to the
object being named. Bounding boxes cannot represent a direction; that is exactly the
information they discard. Whole-body pose — face landmarks for head orientation,
shoulder–elbow–wrist for a pointing ray — can, and 133-keypoint pose estimates exist for
roughly 55% of this corpus's training pairs. So we tested it.

Three families of pose cue — hand presence, *directional* cues (head orientation, arm
extension, showing height), and hands split into **the child's own** (orphan hands entering
low from the frame bottom, no attached body) versus **a partner's** (hands on a person with
a visible face and torso) — are all essentially uncorrelated with CLIP alignment ($\rho \le
0.05$). Because CLIP scores the whole frame, that null is ambiguous — a caregiver pointing at
a small named object is a moment CLIP might miss — so we ran the decisive test: use each cue
as a *training filter* and measure the downstream 4AFC against a CLIP-aligned positive
control (all matched to 8k pairs from the pose-covered pool, three seeds).

| training filter | 4AFC |
|-----------------|------|
| CLIP-aligned (positive control) | 37.9 ± 0.6 |
| random | 28.5 ± 0.3 |
| child's-own hand present | 27.8 ± 1.0 |
| partner's face present | 27.3 ± 1.2 |

The control behaves — CLIP-aligned pairs train **+9 points** over random — so the test has
power. The pose cues do not: filtering to them trains no better than random. The cues are
**not merely invisible to CLIP; they genuinely do not select better learning moments.**

This tightens the chapter's thesis rather than loosening it. The machine-readable social
signal available here — even whole-body pose, even with the child's own hands cleanly
separated from a caregiver's — does not recover reference at this corpus's one-frame-per-
second resolution. But that conclusion rests on the *particular* cues we tried, which were
all forms of *presence*: is a hand, a face, an extended arm in the frame. A closer reading of
what actually determines reference in child-directed speech says those are the weak ones.

## Which cues, and do they combine? {#sec-which}

@frank2013social hand-annotated 24 videos of caregiver–child play and measured, for each cue,
its precision and recall at identifying the object a caregiver was talking about. The ranking
is instructive: a caregiver's **pointing** is highly precise (.78) but rare (recall .10); the
cues that carry the most information are the **child's own gaze** (F = .54) and **discourse
continuity** — whether this utterance continues the topic of the last (F = .53). Hand and
face *presence* — what we tested above — sit near the bottom (precision ~.42). We had been
testing the weak cues.

So we built the others. **Discourse continuity** is transcript-only and sidesteps the
temporal-resolution problem entirely: for each utterance we count the content words it shares
with the utterances in the preceding 20 seconds. **Prosody** — the energy and pitch dynamics
of child-directed speech — comes from the audio. **Child gaze** we approximate by the frame
*centre* (in a head-mounted camera, the centre of the image is where the child is looking).
And we made a genuine attempt at **pointing**: an adult with an extended, straightened arm.

As correlates of CLIP alignment these are all weak, but in a telling pattern. Prosody (energy
range $\rho \approx 0.18$) and discourse continuity ($\rho \approx 0.09$) — the *language-side*
cues — carry real, positive signal. The *vision-side* cues do not: the frame-centre proxy is
slightly negative ($\rho \approx -0.06$), and pointing is null. A combined cross-validated
predictor of all of them reaches $\rho \approx 0.21$ — above the best single cue, so the cues
genuinely add — but still short of the $\rho \approx 0.3$ the titration demands.

Do the language cues select better *learning* moments? Using each as a training filter (three
seeds, against a CLIP-aligned positive control that trains +5 points over random), discourse
and prosody each buy a small, real lift — about +1.5 points over random, roughly a quarter of
what the CLIP control buys — and, exactly as @frank2013social found, the **combination does
not beat the best single cue**: the cues are redundant, and the null vision cues only dilute
it. Folded into the bootstrap itself, as a prior on the EM E-step, a discourse cue lifts the
self-supervised model +1.3 points — the same order as the language prior of @sec-e2e8, and
still far from ignition.

Two findings deserve to be separated, because they fail for different reasons.

- **Pointing is a *detection* failure, not an informativeness one.** Extended straight arms
  are about as rare as @frank2013social's points (~10% of frames), but inspecting them shows
  the detector catching caregivers *leaning and reaching toward the child*, not pointing at a
  distal object; and the finger-level signature of a true point is unresolvable — at arm's
  length the hand keypoints are too small to read. The one cue the literature most wants is
  precisely the one this data's resolution cannot expose.
- **"Looked-at" and "held" are *heuristic* failures.** The developmental logic is sound —
  children talk about what they hold and look at — but our proxies measured the wrong thing:
  a child hand's mere *presence*, and the centre's mere *contrast* with the periphery, rather
  than an *object at the hand* or *at the centre*. Whether a proper "object-there"
  operationalisation recovers the signal is the open question this chapter leaves — and,
  unlike pointing, it is answerable with the data in hand.

The honest summary is a split. **Reference in BabyView is socially cued, but only the
language-side cues — prosody, discourse continuity — are both present in the stream and
machine-readable at 1 fps, and even they are weak: a quarter of the CLIP filter, nowhere near
ignition.** The vision-side cues fail for two separable reasons — the gesture that carries the
most information (pointing) lives below the data's temporal and spatial resolution, and the
cues that should work (holding, looking) were measured too crudely to tell. The titration's
target, a cue worth $\rho \gtrsim 0.3$, remains unmet; but the map of *why* is now specific
enough to say what would move it: object-grounded cues that ask what is *at* the hand or the
centre rather than whether a hand is present, higher-frame-rate gaze and gesture tracking, or
more data to let the rare, precise cues accumulate.
